name: NYC Dept of Environmental Protection (DEP)
tagline: The largest municipal water and wastewater utility in the U.S. — DEP runs NYC's drinking-water supply, sewers, and 14 treatment plants on a sprawling OT/SCADA footprint, while citywide IT policy and procurement run through the Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI).
overview: The New York City Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) manages the city's water supply, wastewater treatment, and water/sewer infrastructure — the largest municipal water utility in the U.S. It delivers more than 1 billion gallons of drinking water a day to roughly 8 million city residents (and additional upstate communities), operates about 6,600 miles of water mains and 7,400+ miles of sewers, and runs 14 wastewater treatment plants handling on the order of 1.3 billion gallons a day, plus 19 reservoirs across a multi-county watershed. DEP's technology profile is two-sided: a deep operational-technology (OT) layer — SCADA, PLCs, HMI, telemetry, and pumping/plant control across all five boroughs — sits alongside enterprise IT for billing, asset management, and customer service. Citywide IT strategy, cybersecurity policy, cloud standards, and much procurement run through the NYC Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI), but DEP retains its own substantial engineering and technology footprint, including a Bureau of Wastewater Treatment 'digital transformation' organized around SCADA support, IT/cybersecurity, asset management, and application development. For a Dell SLG seller, DEP is an OT-modernization and critical-infrastructure-security account that must be navigated alongside OTI's citywide policy and contracting layer.

budget.intro: DEP is funded primarily through the self-supporting NYC water and sewer system — water/wastewater rates set by the NYC Water Board cover operations and capital debt rather than the city's general fund. The FY2026 operating budget is roughly $1.88B, and DEP runs one of the city's largest capital programs, on the order of $33–37B over ten years with capital commitments approaching ~$3B a year.

stats.label: 10-yr capital program
stats.note: Water supply, sewers, and wastewater treatment; ~$3B/yr in commitments

notes: The water/sewer system is self-supporting: rates set by the NYC Water Board fund O&M and capital debt service, so DEP's spend tracks rate revenue and capital commitments rather than a general-fund line.
Large mandated capital work drives the program — e.g., a multi-billion-dollar Newtown Creek wastewater upgrade (~$3.2B) and ongoing state-of-good-repair across treatment plants.
Confirm current-year figures against the adopted city budget and the latest Water Board rate report — capital totals vary by document and cycle.

initiatives.name: Bureau of Wastewater Treatment digital transformation
initiatives.summary: DEP's wastewater bureau is modernizing around four pillars — SCADA support/development, IT support and cybersecurity, asset management, and application development — across 14 treatment plants and 90+ pumping facilities. A direct opening for OT/IT infrastructure, edge compute, and managed services.

initiatives.name: Automated meter upgrade (AMR → AMI)
initiatives.summary: DEP is upgrading 600,000+ automated meter-reading devices citywide toward an advanced metering (AMI) network for real-time usage data and leak detection (network provided by Aclara). Generates data-platform, analytics, and security workloads.

initiatives.name: OT/IT segmentation & water-sector cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: Proposed New York State water/wastewater cyber regulations would require separating OT systems from IT, incident-response plans, and 24-hour incident reporting — pushing utilities like DEP toward network segmentation, OT visibility, and resilience investment.

initiatives.name: Asset management & resiliency
initiatives.summary: Aging mains, sewers, and treatment assets plus stormwater/coastal resiliency mandates drive an asset-management and data-modernization track — sensors, digital records, and analytics to prioritize state-of-good-repair capital.

procurement.intro: Selling to DEP means navigating both NYC's citywide procurement system and OTI's role as the city's IT utility. Confirm Dell's standing on the relevant citywide IT contracts and that any cloud/aaS offering meets OTI's citywide cloud and security policies before pursuing DEP-specific opportunities.

vehicles.name: NYC PASSPort
vehicles.detail: The city's central procurement and vendor system — vendors register, submit responses, and manage contracts through PASSPort. The required front door for most NYC solicitations, including DEP's.

vehicles.name: Citywide OTI / BuyIT contracts
vehicles.detail: OTI sets citywide IT strategy, security policy, and cloud standards and procures citywide IT services; its BuyIT procurement reforms shape how agencies buy technology. Confirm coverage and policy fit here.

vehicles.name: DEP capital & engineering solicitations
vehicles.detail: DEP issues its own large capital/engineering and OT-adjacent contracts via NYC procurement — a route for plant-modernization, SCADA, and infrastructure work tied to the capital program.

powerStructure.name: Zohran Mamdani
powerStructure.title: Mayor, City of New York
powerStructure.note: Sworn in Jan 1, 2026; appoints DEP and OTI leadership and sets citywide budget/capital priorities. Administration is still defining its technology posture — track executive-budget and OTI signals.

powerStructure.name: Lisa Garcia
powerStructure.title: Commissioner, NYC DEP
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Mayor Mamdani (Jan 2026, announced/confirmed through spring); former U.S. EPA Region 2 Administrator. Sets agency priorities — the top relationship at DEP.

powerStructure.name: Lisa Gelobter
powerStructure.title: CTO & Commissioner, NYC Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI)
powerStructure.note: Appointed Feb 2026 (succeeded Matt Fraser); oversees citywide IT infrastructure, cybersecurity, cloud and data policy. Sets the standards and contracting layer above DEP — a key influence relationship.

powerStructure.name: Kelly Moan
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), head of NYC Cyber Command
powerStructure.note: Leads citywide cyber defense across 100+ agencies within OTI. Predates the Mamdani administration — confirm she remains in role. Central for any OT/critical-infrastructure security conversation.

powerStructure.name: DEP Bureau of Wastewater Treatment (BWT)
powerStructure.title: Wastewater operations & digital transformation
powerStructure.note: Owns the SCADA / IT-cyber / asset-management / app-dev modernization across treatment plants — a primary OT buying center.

powerStructure.name: DEP technology / engineering leadership
powerStructure.title: Agency IT, OT & engineering
powerStructure.note: DEP's own IT and engineering organization runs systems, capital projects, and OT — confirm current named leads before forecasting.

news.title: NYC Council adopts ~$115.8B FY2027 budget (June 30) with an expanded five-year capital plan (~$117B, ~$8B in new Mamdani-administration investment)
news.impact: Sets the citywide fiscal envelope DEP's capital and OT work sits within; larger capital plan signals continued infrastructure spend — re-confirm DEP capital lines and emergency-sewer/contract levels.

news.title: NYC Water Board adopts ~6% FY2027 water rate, effective July 1, 2026 (with Home Water Assistance credit, ~4.5% effective)
news.impact: Rate revenue funds DEP's operating and capital plan — a confirmed signal of continued infrastructure investment for the new fiscal year.

news.title: Lisa Gelobter named NYC CTO & OTI Commissioner (succeeding Matt Fraser)
news.impact: New citywide technology, cloud and cybersecurity leadership above DEP — re-map the OTI relationship and confirm any shifts in citywide IT/security standards and procurement.

news.title: NYS water-sector cyber rules: OT/IT separation and incident reporting; OT-focused compliance deadline Jan 1, 2027
news.impact: Near-term compliance driver for DEP — OT/IT segmentation, OT visibility, incident-response and 24-hour reporting. Grant funding available. Reinforce the critical-infrastructure-security message now.

news.title: Lisa Garcia named DEP Commissioner as Aggarwala steps down
news.impact: Agency leadership under Mayor Mamdani (sworn in Jan 1, 2026) — executive priorities and the top relationship at DEP.

news.title: DEP continues citywide AMR-to-AMI meter upgrade (600,000+ devices)
news.impact: Multi-year metering modernization generates data-platform, analytics, and security workloads adjacent to enterprise IT.

dellActions: Lead with critical-infrastructure security and OT/IT segmentation tied to the proposed NYS water/wastewater cyber rules — DEP must separate OT from IT, harden SCADA, and report incidents.
Position ISG infrastructure, rugged/edge compute, and storage for the wastewater bureau's digital transformation and plant modernization across the 14 treatment plants.
Build the metering/data play: AMI generates real-time data needing platforms, analytics, and secure storage — connect Dell data-infrastructure and AI to that workload.
Run a dual-thread account map — DEP's own engineering/OT buying centers plus citywide OTI policy and procurement — and confirm Dell's standing on the relevant NYC IT contracts.
Validate procurement access via PASSPort and OTI/BuyIT, and confirm any cloud/aaS offering meets OTI's citywide cloud and cybersecurity policies before pursuing DEP opportunities.
